narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 36, which is right around the typical level for students in our dataset. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 63.5% chance that you would face challenges and fail it—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower rankings in school deciles for Year 1 and especially Year 3 grades, where you placed in the 2nd and 1st deciles respectively, well below the average 6th decile marks that most successful students achieve. These early and later academic standings had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, signaling to the model potential hurdles in sustaining the high performance needed for the bar. On a brighter note, your LSAT score pushed meaningfully in the opposite direction, toward a passing prediction, nearly offsetting some of those concerns and aligning closely with what top performers typically score. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly under the average of 3.21, offered a modest counterbalance toward success, while your middle family income bracket and choice to attend full-time fell in line with the norm for most students and had minimal sway. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your profile stands out for those lower year deciles, which outweighed your competitive LSAT and set you apart from the stronger academic trajectories that the model favors. We know this outcome can feel disheartening, especially given the unique barriers Black women often navigate in higher education. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. You might also explore programs offering bar prep support or admissions appeals with updated transcripts. Your path to law school is still very much within reach—keep pushing, and we're rooting for you.
